speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] This is roll one with Jeffrey Gradow. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors Survivors of the Shoah--

speaker: interviewer
text: Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. The date is October 29, 1996. The survivor's name is Jeffrey Gradow. The interviewer's name is Merle Goldberg. We are in Los Angeles, California, in the United States. And the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Merle Goldberg. Today is Tuesday, October 29, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Jeffrey Gradow in English, in Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll try my best to look at her. [LAUGHING]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. All right. Ready? First of all, can you tell me your full name as you are called today, and spell all the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Jeffrey Gradow, J-E-F-F-R-E-Y, Gradow, G-R-A-D-O-W.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you give me your full name, as you were give-- the name you were given a birth, and spell all those names for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim Gradowicz. Chaim, C-H-A-I-M, last name, G-R-A-D-O-W-I-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me your full birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: January 5, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what city and country where you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in the city of Mlawa, M-L-A-W-A, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if I were to look at the map of Poland, where would I find Mlawa?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's northwest of Warsaw, the closest city is Poznan, P-O-Z-N-A-N. It's close to the German border, Prussia or 'Prew-sha,' they pronounce it. It used to be a border town.

speaker: interviewer
text: And while you were growing up in Mlawa, did you have any nicknames, or any-- were there any terms of endearment that anyone called you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were just Chaim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, from now on, just-- just to mention, you don't have to spell everything for me now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: The reason you don't have to is that it will break up the interview as-- the flow of it. It'll-- you'll be saying something and all of a sudden you start spelling and it'll be--

speaker: interviewee
text: I see.

speaker: interviewer
text: It will be awful. So, just-- you don't have to spell anything anymore, unless I specifically ask you to do so.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. OK. To begin with, why don't you tell me about your family. I know we had a little discussion about this last week, and I was wondering if you could tell me if you knew how long your family had been in Mlawa, or even in Poland, in this part of the world? On either-- both sides, both maternal and paternal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I can only assume that they were there for generations. Both my parents were born also in Poland. My mother was born, I believe, in 1902, and my father was also born at-- in the, in the same year. Now, there might be a difference in a few months, but I don't know exactly when they were born. Also my grandparents were born in Poland and my uncles-- the whole family-- they were all born in the same city. And I would say at least a few generations. There were some from the family also immigrate to the United States in the beginning of the 19-- of the 20th century, which I met them after the war. But otherwise, I would assume that everybody was living in the same town, or in the vicinity, in maybe 20, 25 miles. My mother's side-- she had another sister, and also family-- and-- a very close-knit family.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you're speaking about someone and-- I should just preface this-- you should always use their name so we'll know-- for instance, you know, if you talk about an aunt, or a, a sister, or brother of either of your parents, always say the name--

speaker: interviewee
text: My--

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, the name of the person, so we don't get confused. You can say, my aunt, but you-- or my uncle, or a sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: But always use a name. As you were talking about your parents and talking about when they were born, could you tell me-- why don't we start with your father? Why don't you tell me his full name and a little bit about him, what he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father, his name was [HEBREW] in Hebrew, or Nochim they call it in Polish. He had two brothers. One was Avrom, or Abraham, the other one was David, which were very close. We used to meet on the-- almost daily, or biweekly, or weekly, in our home. My grandfather was a very religious person. He was Orthodox. So all his life, I would say-- and maybe half of his life, he was in a shul, in temple. And we were an average working family. And-- that was before the war started, actually. I had two sisters, one was Shasna, one was Dina. Dina was the older one. I was the oldest in the family. When I saw her last, she was approximately, either 11 or 12 years old. And my youngest one had to be eight or nine years. The last time I saw my sisters and whole family was in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: But we're getting a little ahead of ourselves here, OK. Let's go back a little bit. You said that your grandfather spent-- this is your father's father-- spent half his life in shul, but you didn't tell us his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Boruch. Boruch Gradowicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know your grandmother, his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't know what happened, I mean, I never saw her.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about on your mother's side? Why don't you tell me your mother's full name, and her maiden name, and a little bit about her. And how--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Fela, or Faiga [NON-ENGLISH]. And she had also a sister, but very seldom-- we saw her only when I was a kid. Her sister and her whole family were living in a small town. I don't even remember the name of it-- not too far. We were not too close because we did not see them. And it's-- all perished in, in the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I was wondering if you could tell me about your father. You said that your grandfather was such a religious man. Did you also come-- live in such a religious house as your grandfather?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what Jewish life was like for you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were average, I would say. On Saturdays, naturally, we had dinner on a Friday night, make kiddish, you know, the blessing. But my father was entirely different than, than my grandfather. He was more progressive, he belonged to, to a labor party, I think it was called, Bund, if I'm not mistaken. It was more like socialist party, I don't know. I'm not too knowledgeable about those things. And before the war, I mean, he had his own shop. He was a shoemaker. And he naturally took repairs, made shoes. And during the week, there was a market, where all the small merchant used to get together. And the farmers used to come, they used to exchange merchandise.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was a happy family. It wasn't a rich family, but average family. Now, the question is what's an average family? In my case, we all lived in a small house on a major street-- it's called [NON-ENGLISH], meaning [NON-ENGLISH] Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were maybe about 3/4 miles from the school-- miles or kilometers-- pardon me. We used to use kilometers, not miles. And naturally I was the oldest one so-- winter, or summer, or spring, rain or shine, we had to walk to school. There was no such thing as transportation to school. And no parents would pick up the kids from school. It was a, a public school, but only Jewish kids went to that school. There was another public school where all the Christian kids went to school. We were entirely different-- we were apart. I mean, it's hard to understand in the US what means-- you got two public school. One for Jewish, and one for Christian. That was the system. And I was a good student. We used to-- we used to-- during certain lessons, we used to go outside, play in the back-- we had the big field, almost like a half a farm, where the kids used to go out. We used to plant all kinds of vegetables, so we understood how we get our food. But the sanitation conditions were not too hard during that time. We had to walk at least maybe 500 feet or more to a bathroom. And it was just made out of boards, and that was acceptable. My teacher, it was the same teacher-- I had her from the first grade till, till the seventh grade. It's not like in the United States. They keep changing it. The professor from the school was the same one for all those years I went to school. And the school, it was on the same street where I used to live. Now, during holidays we used to get together, my uncles, my grandfather, but I never saw my grandmother, so I don't know what happened. Either they were separated, or she passed away, I have no way of knowing. But it was a happy family, until the war broke out. Before the war even. We used to see, at least two years before, since it was border town-- north of us was [NON-ENGLISH], or Prussia, whatever they called it. It was part of Germany. And two years before, the Polish Army was building almost like a Maginot Line. We couldn't understand what's going on. We could see big trucks with cement going day and night, until the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: But before the war broke out-- let me ask you, what did your mother do? You didn't say that. You--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. My mother was a housewife. She did not work. She was taking care of the kids. And living in a town-- it's primitive. She had to cook, and wash, and clean. There's no such thing as going to a market and buy all the stuff. She had to walk, maybe a few hundred yards to buy us a bread or challah. We had stores in the city, but some people could afford it, and some could not afford it. We were lucky at that point. My father did make enough that we could live comfortable.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for instance, you know, getting back to Shabbos, for instance, in the preparations for Shabbos, did your mother make the challah, or did you go to the bakery? Or how did it-- how did the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Everything was cooked and baked at home. They had the old-fashioned ovens, and she used to-- she used to bake the challah and what else, [INAUDIBLE]. She used to-- certain food item-- used to put it in the oven and prepared for Saturday-- for Friday night and Saturday. Because mostly on Saturday, we had to eat cold because he could not warm up, even though we're not too religious, but my mother would not dare to warm up, or cook anything, on Saturdays because my grandfather kept an eye on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, is that her father or your father's father?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It's my father's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about--

speaker: interviewee
text: My, my mother's family, I knew very little of it because, I don't know. It was very hard with transportation to get-- so we just stayed away. A small city maybe would take maybe an hour, an hour and a half walking at time, but very seldom we would see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they live? In what city? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a small town. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a shtetl, or was it really--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a-- well, I don't know, it's called a shtetl, or whatever. But it was very small. I don't even-- I never paid any attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your-- what kind of kosher laws did your-- did your mother keep?

speaker: interviewee
text: We observed strictly kosher laws. You know, if you had chicken, you had to go to-- to the, what do you call it? The Shochet. And it had to be kosher, I mean, naturally. Now, if something happens, I have no way of knowing. No knowledge of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wonder, were you bar mitzvahed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your bar mitzvah like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bar mitzvah was very simple. It's not like over here, like in the United States. On Saturday, you say the prayer of [HEBREW] and the older people got some glass of wine. They had some herring. And-- it's, it's simple, just like you would-- there was no big deal. We never made out any big deal over it. And that was the whole, the whole bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no dancing or singing to do. Just religious service and that's the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father give you any kind of advice on your bar mitzvah day, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was not too, too religious. I don't know, it's such a big difference between my father and grandfather. It's unbelievable. And I never got any advice, as far as religion is concerned, or anything else.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about from your grandfather on your bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandfather was a different story, but I used to go to [NON-ENGLISH]. So naturally, he was more interested in, in checking on me. If I go to [NON-ENGLISH] or I study. And he told me, remember. You have to go ever, every Saturday at least, you have to go to the temple. But as far as mixing in our family life, he never did. He stayed out.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about anti-Semitism in Mlawa when you were growing up? This is before the war, I'm talking.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it-- did you encounter, or witness any kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we did. Oh, that's right. They had-- first of all, they had separate schools to keep the kids apart. There was some fighting, even, during the market day. Well, there, there used to be some really anti-Semitic Poles, and they will try to destroy the Jewish merchants. Throw-- not hurting them physically, but throw down the tables. They used to have a table and on the table used to be shoes, or clothing, or anything else. They used to just knock it down. The police stayed out. But there were also some left-wing Poles, which came in. And before you know, it was starting a whole fight, used to be. Not very often, but once in a while it happens.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually ever witnessed this? Or was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was afraid. I just ran away. When we could see-- I used to come over to see my father, but when they made a noise like that, I took off. I was the first running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you said that your family was comfortable, I guess--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Was your mother very philanthropic? I mean, on the holidays, was there tzedakah? Did she invite poor Jewish families in to, to, to eat with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too often. But from time to time, we used to have some guests in our house. If they're poor or rich, I have no way of knowing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about a radio? Did you have a radio?

speaker: interviewee
text: What's a radio? We didn't know what a radio means. We have no radio, no television. There's no such thing as a radio. We didn't have-- let's put it this way, in our family we never had a radio.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about, was there any Yiddish theater in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a famous theater. They used to come from Warsaw. What's the name? It's a Yiddish theater. I don't remember exactly what. But once in awhile because there was a big Jewish population. I would say maybe 50% or more, were Jews in Mlawa. And also there's one famous Jewish writer, Opatoshu. He was born in Mlawa. But this-- all this happened before my time, but they were talking always about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, in terms of the Yiddish theater, there must have been Yiddish life--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There were lots of organizations, Zionist organization, Bund organization. There were some also extreme left organization, like a communist party, whatever they-- because when they had the war in Spain, the Civil War, I remember my dad told me that some, some of the people went to fight even in Spain. Not-- there were a few, a few young men, volunteered to fight in Spain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Jewish men? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish men, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But I just know from what my father told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I've never heard that before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. And also there were some Christian also. There must have been a communist-- I don't know exactly. I couldn't say it. But I know that some of the people went to fight in Spain, young men.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like the Americans did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, there were Americans. They were called the Lincoln Brigade. So it was the same idea, that--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was  an international brigade.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, now that you're speaking of 1936--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: The reason I asked you you have-- if you had a radio was because some people did.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And If they did, there were things they heard on the radio. That's what I was trying to get at.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't know it. All I know it is from my father told us, I think, once at a dinner time, that certain people, which he know about it, went to Spain.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since you lived so close to Germany, obviously once Hitler came to power, there most have been a great many German Jews who were trying to get out. Did they come through Mlawa?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. You see, I would say, approximately 25% or 30% of Polish citizen were German descent. They're called volksdeutsche. And we didn't know about it. We know there was a church, a German church, there was a Catholic church, and there was a temple. We had also small-- they had temples, which the religious Jews used to sit over there, day and night. I mean, it was unbelievable. We used to-- as kids, we used to run over and see what's going on over there, but we never dared to go inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, what I guess-- but you don't remember people coming through, German Jews, coming through?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Once-- after 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was too young. I wouldn't know about it. Even Mlawa was a major thoroughfare from Eastern Europe to the Baltic Sea, which is Gdynia or Gdansk. Gdansk, or Gdynia, supposedly is supposed to be a free city, but unfortunately it was, it was mostly German, because I met some people who were living in Gdansk after the war, and they told me, it's just like in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know you didn't have a radio, and you didn't-- and there were no TVs in those days, but were there-- were there movie theaters in your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had one movie theater.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go to the movies and see newsreels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I once remember I didn't have the money to go in so the door was open a little bit in the summertime-- there was no air conditioning-- and that's the first time in my life I saw a black woman on the, on the screen. I couldn't understand-- all of us, all kids, there were about six or seven kids-- we always looked in. We shout, take a look. There's a black woman over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't know what it means because we never saw any, any black people. And it was also, what's her name? She played in a-- as a child in a famous-- she was in the United Nation. I forgot the name of her. Oh, my gosh. Shirley Temple?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shirley Temple, yes. At that was the film. And I do, even today, I remember that's the first time we saw it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in the movie theaters where newsreel-- if newsreels were shown, did you see newsreels about what was-- did you see how-- film about Germany, and Hitler coming--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never saw anything. In fact, we didn't know what was going on. I didn't know. Let's put it this way. I couldn't speak for somebody else. We were too young. We were playing soccer, that was our game, and in winter time-- you know, it was cold over the ice. So I made our own sled. And we played around with kids. We didn't think about politics or anything else. We were too young for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your sisters? What did you do with your-- what did you and sisters play?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my littles-- were young, little sisters. I mean, very little. They were staying with mother. They were always very attached to, to Mom. Once in awhile, they used to come over to me. We used to go and play-- try to play soccer. We never played any actually. But they were little kids, I mean. And it's not-- they didn't have the luxury of, of, of going out. Or once a while we used to go to the park. There was a park in the middle of the city. We used to walk around with our parents. But otherwise, nothing special. I can-- now, after 50 years, I can hardly remember. All I remember, my little one, she used to stick to me like glue. She used to grab me by my slacks, short slacks, in summertime. But otherwise, I-- very little I now remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Since your family was, was comfortable, in that summer before Poland was invaded, in that summer of '39, was your-- did your family take a vacation together? Did you go any place, like Zakopane? Did you do anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. But I used to go with the kids to a camp. We made a Jewish camp. Is was maybe three, four kilometers from the city. We could walk there. All the way around the city were woods, heavy woods, mostly palm-- not-- Christmas trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fir trees.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And also birch trees. And there-- that's the place we used stay for, for two, three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it a sleepover camp, or was it a day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a sleepover camp, but very primitive. They had small-- made out almost of plywood, or whatever it was. And we were sleeping about eight, 10 kids in one place. But we had supervision.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sisters also attended the camp or no?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older one attended, but not the younger one. My older sister, which was almost nine or 10 years old, approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name again?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, your sister's name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Dina.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was the older one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the older one. Shasna was the, the youngest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the camp? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no name of them. We would just go to Jewish camp. Camp out of the city, that's all. There were no name. It was organized by the-- the rabbi that was over there. Of course, we had a rabbi from cheder, and he supervised us. He was a teacher, actually. Young man, very progressive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the September of '39, what happened next? Where were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at home, and early in the morning we had a horrible explosion. It was-- this I'll never forget-- it was September 1st. An artillery bomb exploded a few hundred yards from our house. We didn't know what's happening. Everybody was out-- then we saw the Polish soldiers going closer to the border, going up north. There was the major thoroughfare, so they used to-- they used to go over there. And we realized, from mouth to mouth, that the Germans invaded Poland. And they built the fortification before the war-- so the Germans didn't-- was not-- didn't come into Mlawa first. They were almost close to Warsaw. And we were encircled, but they came from the other side, instead of breaking through, or go straight ahead. They didn't do it. They came from other side. They were close to Warsaw and then they come back to us. And then we saw troops are coming in from the opposite side, not from the German border. Like, for example, from the north side, north of us was the German, German border. They came from the south side on the major boulevard. Light, tanks drove in. And we couldn't understand what's going on. Everybody was afraid, for sitting in a home. And beginning, we tried to get out of the house, and run to the fields, but it was worse yet. So we got stuck over there in a farmer's house. And that was the beginning of World War II. But I, I went through two wars, not more war. Because one war started September 1, 1939, and it was-- Poland was partitioned, east and west. The eastern part, the Soviet Union took over, and the western part the German took over. And we were under German occupation already.

speaker: interviewer
text: But why don't we just talk about you personally. You don't have to divide the war this way. We're going to stop and change the reel. Go on. OK.
